Project: Introduction to Neural Networks: ReneWind¶

Context¶

Renewable energy sources play an increasingly important role in the global energy mix, as the effort to reduce the environmental impact of energy production increases.

Out of all the renewable energy alternatives, wind energy is one of the most developed technologies worldwide. The U.S. Department of Energy has put together a guide to achieving operational efficiency using predictive maintenance practices.

Predictive maintenance uses sensor information and analysis methods to measure and predict degradation and future component capability. The idea behind predictive maintenance is that failure patterns are predictable and if component failure can be predicted accurately and the component is replaced before it fails, the costs of operation and maintenance will be much lower.

The sensors fitted across different machines involved in the process of energy generation collect data related to various environmental factors (temperature, humidity, wind speed, etc.) and additional features related to various parts of the wind turbine (gearbox, tower, blades, break, etc.).

Objective¶

“ReneWind” is a company working on improving the machinery/processes involved in the production of wind energy using machine learning and has collected data on generator failure of wind turbines using sensors. They have shared a ciphered version of the data, as the data collected through sensors is confidential (the type of data collected varies with companies). Data has 40 predictors, 20000 observations in the training set, and 5000 in the test set. The objective is to build various classification models, tune them, and find the best one that will help identify failures so that the generators can be repaired before failing/breaking to reduce the overall maintenance cost.

The nature of predictions made by the classification model will translate as follows:

  • True positives (TP) are failures correctly predicted by the model. These will result in repair costs.
  • False negatives (FN) are real failures where there is no detection by the model. These will result in replacement costs.
  • False positives (FP) are detections where there is no failure. These will result in inspection costs.

It is given that the cost of repairing a generator is much less than the cost of replacing it, and the cost of inspection is less than the cost of repair.

Note:

  • Inspection Cost < Repair Cost < Replacement Cost
  • “1” in the target variable should be considered as “failure” and “0” represents “No failure”.

Data Description¶

The data provided is a transformed version of the original data which was collected using sensors.

  • Train.csv - To be used for training and tuning of models.
  • Test.csv - To be used only for testing the performance of the final best model.

Both datasets consist of 40 predictor variables and 1 target variable.

Key Goals Or Problems to be solved:¶

The objective is to

  1. Solve: Build various classification models, tune them, and find the best one that will help identify failures so that the generators can be repaired before failing/breaking to reduce the overall maintenance cost.
  • Note: “1” in the target variable should be considered as “failure” and “0” represents “No failure”.
  1. Evaluate: The nature of predictions made by the classification model will translate as follows:
  • True positives (TP) are failures correctly predicted by the model. These will result in repair costs.
  • False negatives (FN) are real failures where there is no detection by the model. These will result in replacement costs.
  • False positives (FP) are detections where there is no failure. These will result in inspection costs.
  1. Optimize: It is given that :
  • Inspection Cost < Repair Cost < Replacement Cost

Importing the required libraries¶

In [3]:
#Installing the libraries with specific versions
!pip install  tensorflow==2.18.0 scikit-learn==1.3.2 matplotlib===3.8.3 seaborn==0.13.2 numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.2   -q --user --no-warn-script-location
In [4]:
# To help with reading and manipulation of data
import numpy as np
from numpy import array
import pandas as pd

# To help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# To split the data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# To impute missing values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

# To do one-hot encoding
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

# Module for time-related operations.
import time

#Modules for Neural Network Model building
import tensorflow as tf #An end-to-end open source machine learning platform
from tensorflow import keras  # High-level neural networks API for deep learning.
from keras import backend   # Abstraction layer for neural network backend engines.
from keras.models import Sequential  # Model for building NN sequentially.
from keras.layers import Dense,Dropout,BatchNormalization   # for creating fully connected neural network layers.

# To get different performance metrics
import sklearn.metrics as metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    classification_report,
    confusion_matrix,
    recall_score,
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    f1_score,
)
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

# To undersample and oversample the data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold

# To suppress warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Data Overview¶

Loading the Data and Reviewing it¶

In [5]:
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Mounted at /content/drive
In [6]:
# loading Training data into a dataframe
path='/content/drive/MyDrive/Learnings/Python_Saved_Path/ReneWind-Train.csv'
reneWind=pd.read_csv(path)

# creating a copy of the Training data
data_train=reneWind.copy()

# loading Test data into a dataframe
path='/content/drive/MyDrive/Learnings/Python_Saved_Path/ReneWind-Test.csv'
reneWindTest=pd.read_csv(path)

# creating a copy of the Test data
data_test=reneWindTest.copy()

Observations¶

  • Data Dimensions: The training dataset (data_train) contains 20,000 observations and 41 columns (V1-V40 and Target).
  • Data Types: All predictor variables (V1-V40) are of float64 type, while the target variable (Target) is of int64 type.
  • Target Variable Imbalance: The 'Target' variable has a mean of approximately 0.0555, indicating that only about 5.55% of the training data represents 'failure' (class 1), suggesting a significant class imbalance.
  • Missing Values: Columns V1 and V2 in the data_train DataFrame each have 18 missing values.

11 rows × 41 columns

In [18]:
# Checking if any of the rows with null values had Target as 1
data_test[data_test.isna().any(axis=1)]["Target"].sum()
Out[18]:
np.int64(0)

data_train.duplicated().sum()
Out[19]:
np.int64(0)
In [20]:
data_test.duplicated().sum()
Out[20]:
np.int64(0)

Observations¶

Missing Data:

  • Training Data: There are 18 missing values in V1 column and 18 missing in the V2 column in the Training Data.
    • The missing records all have "No Failure" in the Target variable
    • The rows with missing data constitute 0.18% of the training data
  • Test Data: There 5 missing values in V1 and 6 missing values in the V2 column in the Test Data
    • The missing records all have "No Failure" in the Target variable
    • The rows with missing data constitute 0.11% of the test data

Duplicate Data:

  • There are no duplicate records in the training or test data.

Next Steps:

  • All the independent variables are of type float. The Target variable has only 2 valid values (0 and 1) and hence is being converted into a categorical column.
  • The missing data seem to be random missing data and forms a very small percentage of the datasets, and hence are unlikely to impact the model predictions significantly.
  • If EDA confirms the randomness of the missing data, the missing values can be imputed with the median values of the relevant column of the training data. This would be done at a later stage in a way to ensure data leakage is prevented.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Univariate Analysis¶

In [25]:
# Beginning the Analysis by clearly identifying the numerical and categorical columns
print(f"Numerical Columns: {numerical}")
print(f"Categorical Columns: {categorical}")

Numerical Columns: ['V1', 'V2', 'V3', 'V4', 'V5', 'V6', 'V7', 'V8', 'V9', 'V10', 'V11', 'V12', 'V13', 'V14', 'V15', 'V16', 'V17', 'V18', 'V19', 'V20', 'V21', 'V22', 'V23', 'V24', 'V25', 'V26', 'V27', 'V28', 'V29', 'V30', 'V31', 'V32', 'V33', 'V34', 'V35', 'V36', 'V37', 'V38', 'V39', 'V40']
Categorical Columns: ['Target']
In [27]:
#Boxplot of all numerical features
plt.figure(figsize=(30,30))
plt.suptitle('Univariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
for i, feature in enumerate(numerical):
    plt.subplot(10,4,i+1)
    sns.boxplot(data_train,x=feature)
    plt.title(feature)
    plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(30,20))
plt.suptitle('Univariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
for i, feature in enumerate(numerical):
    plt.subplot(10,4,i+1)
    sns.histplot(data_train,x=feature,hue='Target', kde=True)
    plt.title(feature)
    plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [29]:
#Getting the value counts of 0 and 1 for Target column and plotting this
data_train["Target"].value_counts().plot(kind="bar")
plt.xlabel("Target")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Target Column Value Counts")
#plt to show the values of the bars
for i, v in enumerate(data_train["Target"].value_counts()):
    plt.text(i, v, str(v), ha='center', va='bottom')
plt.show()

Observations¶

Based on the univariate analysis of the numerical and categorical features, here are some key observations:

Univariate Analysis of Numerical Features (V1-V40):

  • Boxplots: Many numerical features exhibit a wide spread and numerous outliers, indicating variability and the presence of extreme values in the data.

  • Histograms: For most features, the distributions for Target '0' (No failure) and Target '1' (failure) show significant overlap. While some features might have subtle differences in their distributions between the two classes, no clear separation is immediately apparent, suggesting that individual features alone may not strongly distinguish between failure and non-failure.

  • Bar plot of Target (Categorical) variable: Target Variable Distribution: There are 18,890 instances of 'No failure' (Target = 0) and 1,110 instances of 'failure' (Target = 1) in the training dataset. This confirms the significant class imbalance, where 'failure' cases constitute only about 5.5% of the data.

These observations provide initial insights into potential drivers of the failure detection, which will be further explored in bivariate analysis and model predictions and analysis.

Bivariate Analysis¶

In [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(20,20))
plt.suptitle('Bivariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
sns.heatmap(data_train[numerical].corr(),annot=True,vmin=-1,vmax=1, fmt='.2f',cmap='Spectral')
plt.show()
In [31]:
plt.figure(figsize=(15,20))
plt.suptitle('Bivariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
for i, feature in enumerate(numerical):
    plt.subplot(10,4,i+1)
    sns.boxplot(data_train,y=feature,x='Target')
    plt.title(feature)
    plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [32]:
#Creating a pair plot of all variables
#plt.figure(figsize=(30,30))
#sns.pairplot(data_train,hue='Target')
#plt.show()

Observations¶

Here are the observations from the bivariate analysis:

  • Correlation Heatmap: The heatmap shows the correlation coefficients between all numerical features. Given that the values are mostly small, there doesn't appear to be strong linear correlation between most of the predictor variables themselves. This could be beneficial for some models as it might indicate less multicollinearity.
  • Boxplots of Numerical Features vs. Target : The boxplots comparing each numerical feature (V1-V40) against the Target variable (failure vs. no failure) generally show a large overlap in the distributions. This indicates that for most individual features, there isn't a clear separation or strong distinguishing pattern between the 'failure' (1) and 'no failure' (0) classes. This reinforces the idea from univariate analysis that single features might not be highly discriminative on their own.
  • Pairplot: An attempt was made to generate a pairplot for all variables, colored by the 'Target' variable. However, this operation had to be aborted due to its high computational intensity. This suggests that with 40 numerical features, a full pairplot for 20,000 observations is too resource-intensive to execute effectively, indicating that more targeted visualization or dimensionality reduction might be needed if detailed pairwise relationships are critical. In the current scenario, it doesn't seem that significant, and hence, would not be pursued further.

Data Pre-processing¶

Encoding of Data¶

Please Note¶

  • The Target column is categorical with only 0 and 1 as values. Hence it doesn't require encoding. Converting it back into int column for neural network model building.
  • No other categorical columns exist.
In [64]:
#Converting Target variable back to int column
data_train["Target"]=data_train["Target"].astype("int")
data_test["Target"]=data_test["Target"].astype("int")
data_train.info()

Splitting the data before building the model¶

Splitting the Data into Training, Validation sets.

In [65]:
# Separating the independent and dependent variables
X_train = data_train.drop(["Target"], axis=1)
y_train = data_train["Target"]

X_test = data_test.drop(["Target"], axis=1)
y_test = data_test["Target"]

# Splitting data into training and validation sets:

# We'll split training data into 2 parts, say validation and training
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_train, y_train, test_size=1/5, random_state=1, stratify=y_train
)


#Printing the shape of the dataset.
print(X_train.shape)
print(y_train.shape)
print(X_val.shape)
print(y_val.shape)
print(X_test.shape)
print(y_test.shape)

(16000, 40)
(16000,)
(4000, 40)
(4000,)
(5000, 40)
(5000,)

Creating Dummy Variables, Imputation of Missing Variables, Etc AFTER Splitting Training, Validation and Test DataSets to avoid Data Leak¶

Scaling the Data¶

In [107]:
# Normalizing the data in each numerical column in X_train, X_val and X_test in a loop
for col in numerical:
    min_val = X_train[col].min()
    max_val = X_train[col].max()

    if (max_val - min_val) == 0:
        # If max and min are the same, all values are identical. Set them to a constant after scaling (e.g., 0.5)
        X_train[col] = 0.5
        X_val[col] = 0.5
        X_test[col] = 0.5
    else:
        # Apply Min-Max scaling
        X_train[col] = (X_train[col] - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
        X_val[col] = (X_val[col] - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
        X_test[col] = (X_test[col] - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)

print(X_train.head())
print(X_val.head())
print(X_test.head())

# Add a check to confirm no NaNs after scaling
print("\nNaNs in X_train after scaling:", X_train.isna().sum().sum())
print("NaNs in X_val after scaling:", X_val.isna().sum().sum())
print("NaNs in X_test after scaling:", X_test.isna().sum().sum())

[5 rows x 40 columns]

NaNs in X_train after scaling: 30
NaNs in X_val after scaling: 6
NaNs in X_test after scaling: 11

Imputing Missing Values¶

In [108]:
v1_train_median=X_train["V1"].median()
v2_train_median=X_train["V2"].median()

#Imputing the median value of V1 and V2 from the Training Data into the training, validation and test data for missing values
X_train["V1"].fillna(v1_train_median , inplace=True)
X_val["V1"].fillna(v1_train_median , inplace=True)
X_test["V1"].fillna(v1_train_median , inplace=True)

X_train["V2"].fillna(v2_train_median, inplace=True)
X_val["V2"].fillna(v2_train_median, inplace=True)
X_test["V2"].fillna(v2_train_median, inplace=True)

#checking for null values in X_train, X_val and X_test
if(X_train.isna().sum().sum()==0 and X_val.isna().sum().sum()==0 and X_test.isna().sum().sum()==0):
  print("No null values in X_train, X_val and X_test")

No null values in X_train, X_val and X_test
In [42]:
print(X_train.shape[0], "train samples")
print(X_val.shape[0], "validation samples")
print(X_test.shape[0], "test samples")

16000 train samples
4000 validation samples
5000 test samples
In [70]:
y_train.sample(5)
Out[70]:
Target
5656 1
1582 0
15199 0
13839 0
18945 0

Summarizing the above¶

  • Data Splitting: The original training data (data_train) was divided into training (X_train, y_train) and validation (X_val, y_val) sets using a 80/20 split, with stratify=y_train to maintain the proportion of target classes. A separate test set (X_test, y_test) was also prepared from data_test. This ensures proper evaluation of model generalization.
  • Data Scaling: All numerical features (V1-V40) in X_train, X_val, and X_test were normalized using Min-Max scaling. Importantly, the minimum and maximum values used for scaling were calculated only from the X_train dataset. This prevents data leakage from the validation and test sets into the training process.
  • Missing Value Imputation: Missing values in columns V1 and V2 were imputed using the median values. Similar to scaling, these median values were calculated only from the X_train dataset and then applied consistently across X_train, X_val, and X_test to prevent data leakage.
  • Data Leakage Prevention: Both scaling and imputation methods were carefully applied using parameters derived solely from the training data, effectively preventing data leakage and ensuring that the evaluation on validation and test sets is unbiased.
  • Final Data Integrity: After imputation, it was confirmed that there are no remaining null values in X_train, X_val, or X_test.

Utility Functions¶

In [47]:
def plot(history, name):
    """
    Function to plot loss/accuracy

    history: an object which stores the metrics and losses.
    name: can be one of Loss or Accuracy
    """
    fig, ax = plt.subplots() #Creating a subplot with figure and axes.
    plt.plot(history.history[name]) #Plotting the train accuracy or train loss
    plt.plot(history.history['val_'+name]) #Plotting the validation accuracy or validation loss

    plt.title('Model ' + name.capitalize()) #Defining the title of the plot.
    plt.ylabel(name.capitalize()) #Capitalizing the first letter.
    plt.xlabel('Epoch') #Defining the label for the x-axis.
    fig.legend(['Train', 'Validation'], loc="outside right upper") #Defining the legend, loc controls the position of the legend.

Dataframe to store the results from all the models we build

In [134]:
#Defining the columns of the dataframe which are nothing but the hyper parameters and the metrics.
columns = ["# hidden layers","# neurons - hidden layer","activation function - hidden layer ","# epochs","batch size","optimizer","class weights","learning rate, momentum","weight initializer","regularization","train loss","validation loss","train accuracy","validation accuracy","time (secs)"]

#Creating a pandas dataframe.
results = pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)
In [135]:
results.info()

memory usage: 132.0+ bytes

Model Building¶

Model 0¶

We'll start out with a baseline model having the following configuration:

  • 1 input, 1 hidden (64 nodes), 1 output layers
  • relu activations for the hidden layer
  • Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
In [109]:
batch_size = 1000
epochs = 30
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [110]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [111]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.SGD()    # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [112]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 64)             │         2,624 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            65 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Total params: 2,689 (10.50 KB)

 Trainable params: 2,689 (10.50 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)
In [113]:
model_1.compile(loss=bce_logits_loss, optimizer=optimizer, metrics=["accuracy"])
In [114]:
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs)
end=time.time()

#Add an entry to results

results.loc[0] = [1,[64],["relu"],30,1000,"sgd","-","-","-","-",history.history["loss"][-1],history.history["val_loss"][-1],history.history["accuracy"][-1],history.history["val_accuracy"][-1],round(end-start,2)]
In [158]:
results
Out[158]:
# hidden layers # neurons - hidden layer activation function - hidden layer # epochs batch size optimizer class weights learning rate, momentum weight initializer regularization train loss validation loss train accuracy validation accuracy time (secs)
0 1 [64] [relu] 30 1000 sgd - - - - 0.210152 0.209642 0.9445 0.9445 6.95

Observations¶

Here are the observations and notes for Model 0:

Model Architecture: This baseline model consists of:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). 1 hidden layer with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True).

Training Configuration:

Epochs: 30 Batch Size: 1000 Optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)

Performance Metrics (after 30 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.210 Validation Loss: ~0.210 Training Accuracy: ~0.9445 Validation Accuracy: ~0.9445 Time Taken: ~6.95 seconds

Observations: The training and validation accuracy quickly stabilized around 0.9445. Given the class imbalance observed earlier (approximately 94.45% 'No Failure' cases), this suggests that the model is primarily predicting the majority class. It is not effectively learning to distinguish the minority 'failure' class. This is common with imbalanced datasets and indicates that further techniques (like class weighting, different architectures, or data resampling) will be needed to improve its ability to detect failures.

In [145]:
from sklearn.utils import class_weight

Model 1¶

We'll keep the same model but with class weights (1:5) having the following configuration:

1 input, 1 hidden (64 nodes), 1 output layers relu activations for the hidden layer Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

In [139]:
batch_size = 1000
epochs = 30
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [140]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [141]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.SGD()    # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [142]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩

In [144]:
model_1.compile(loss=bce_logits_loss, optimizer=optimizer, metrics=["accuracy"])
In [146]:
class_weights = {0: 1.0, 1: 5.0}
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , class_weight=class_weights, batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs)
end=time.time()

16/16 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 8ms/step - accuracy: 0.9438 - loss: 0.5717 - val_accuracy: 0.9445 - val_loss: 0.2893
In [147]:

Time taken in seconds  7.64680552482605

plot(history,'accuracy')

#Add an entry to results

results.loc[1] = [1,64,"relu",30,1000,"sgd","1:5","-","-","-",history.history["loss"][-1],history.history["val_loss"][-1],history.history["accuracy"][-1],history.history["val_accuracy"][-1],round(end-start,2)]
In [160]:
results
Out[160]:
# hidden layers # neurons - hidden layer activation function - hidden layer # epochs batch size optimizer class weights learning rate, momentum weight initializer regularization train loss validation loss train accuracy validation accuracy time (secs)
0 1 [64] [relu] 30 1000 sgd - - - - 0.210152 0.209642 0.9445 0.9445 6.95
1 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 1:5 - - - 0.567296 0.289252 0.9445 0.9445 7.65

Observations¶

Observations and notes for Model 1:

Model Architecture: This model is identical to Model 0, consisting of:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). 1 hidden layer with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True). Training Configuration:

Epochs: 30 Batch Size: 1000 Optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True) Class Weights: {0: 1.0, 1: 5.0} (applied to address class imbalance) Performance Metrics (after 30 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.567 Validation Loss: ~0.289 Training Accuracy: ~0.9445 Validation Accuracy: ~0.9445 Time Taken: ~7.65 seconds

Observations: After applying class weights to penalize misclassifications of the minority class ('failure') more heavily, the overall accuracy for both training and validation sets remained at approximately 0.9445, similar to Model 0. This suggests that while the loss values changed significantly (training loss increased, validation loss slightly increased), the basic accuracy metric is still dominated by the correct prediction of the majority class. The class weights have influenced the model's learning process, as evidenced by the higher training loss, but a simple accuracy metric does not fully capture the improvement (or lack thereof) in predicting the minority class. Further evaluation with metrics like recall, precision, or F1-score specifically for the positive class would be necessary to truly understand the impact of class weighting on detecting failures.

In [ ]:

Model 2¶

We'll keep the same model but with class weights (0.5:9) having the following configuration:

1 input, 1 hidden (64 nodes), 1 output layers relu activations for the hidden layer Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

In [161]:
batch_size = 1000
epochs = 30
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [162]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [163]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.SGD()    # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [164]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 64)             │         2,624 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            65 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Trainable params: 2,689 (10.50 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)
In [165]:
model_1.compile(loss=bce_logits_loss, optimizer=optimizer, metrics=["accuracy"])
In [166]:
class_weights = {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0}
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , class_weight=class_weights, batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs)
end=time.time()

Out[171]:
# hidden layers # neurons - hidden layer activation function - hidden layer # epochs batch size optimizer class weights learning rate, momentum weight initializer regularization train loss validation loss train accuracy validation accuracy time (secs)
0 1 [64] [relu] 30 1000 sgd - - - - 0.210152 0.209642 0.94450 0.9445 6.95
1 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 1:5 - - - 0.567296 0.289252 0.94450 0.9445 7.65
2 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 0.5:9 - - - 0.584882 0.626594 0.95325 0.9540 5.61

Observations¶

Observations and notes for Model 2:

Model Architecture: This model is identical to Model 0 and Model 1, consisting of:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). 1 hidden layer with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True). Training Configuration:

Epochs: 30 Batch Size: 1000 Optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True) Class Weights: {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0} (applied with an even heavier penalty for misclassifying the minority class) Performance Metrics (after 30 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.585 Validation Loss: ~0.627 Training Accuracy: ~0.953 Validation Accuracy: ~0.954 Time Taken: ~5.61 seconds

Observations: With a further adjusted class weight ratio (0: 0.5, 1: 9.0), the model's accuracy on both the training and validation sets has slightly increased to approximately 0.953 and 0.954 respectively. The training loss (0.585) is higher than Model 0, but the validation loss (0.627) is also considerably higher than both Model 0 and Model 1. While the overall accuracy shows a slight improvement, indicating more correct predictions overall, it's still crucial to assess performance using metrics specifically designed for imbalanced datasets, such as recall, precision, and F1-score for the minority class. The increased loss values suggest that the model is being heavily influenced by the new class weights, likely aiming to reduce false negatives for the minority class, which might come at the cost of overall loss.

In [ ]:

Model 3¶

We'll modify the model [ with class weights (1:5)] having the following configuration:

1 input, 2 hidden (128,64 nodes), 1 output layers relu, relu activations for the hidden layer Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

Epoch: 50 Batch Size: 500

In [173]:
batch_size = 500
epochs = 50
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [174]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [175]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.SGD()    # defining SGD as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(128,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu"))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [176]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓

end=time.time()

0 1 [64] [relu] 30 1000 sgd - - - - 0.210152 0.209642 0.94450 0.9445 6.95
1 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 1:5 - - - 0.567296 0.289252 0.94450 0.9445 7.65
2 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 0.5:9 - - - 0.584882 0.626594 0.95325 0.9540 5.61
3 2 128,64 relu,relu 50 500 sgd 0.5:9 - - - 0.368593 0.372975 0.91625 0.9095 14.44

Observations¶

Observations and notes for Model 3:

Model Architecture: This model features a deeper architecture compared to the previous models:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). 2 hidden layers: the first with 128 neurons and relu activation, and the second with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True).

Training Configuration:

Epochs: 50 Batch Size: 500 Optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True) Class Weights: {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0} (same as Model 2)

Performance Metrics (after 50 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.369 Validation Loss: ~0.373 Training Accuracy: ~0.916 Validation Accuracy: ~0.9095 Time Taken: ~14.44 seconds

Observations: Increasing the model complexity (adding a hidden layer with more neurons) and the number of epochs (to 50) while using the same class weights as Model 2 has resulted in a different performance profile. Both training and validation accuracy have decreased compared to Model 2 (from ~0.95 to ~0.91). Similarly, both training and validation losses have increased significantly compared to Model 0 and Model 1, though they are lower than Model 2's validation loss. The training time has also increased due to the larger model and more epochs. The decrease in accuracy and the relatively high loss values suggest that simply adding more layers and neurons with the same class weighting might not be improving the model's ability to discriminate between classes, or it could be struggling to converge effectively with the current optimizer and learning rate. Further investigation into more specialized metrics for imbalanced datasets and hyperparameter tuning (especially learning rate or a different optimizer) for this deeper architecture would be beneficial.

Model 4¶

We'll use the same model [ with class weights (1:5)] but with the Adam optimizer having the following configuration:

1 input, 2 hidden (128,64 nodes), 1 output layers relu, relu activations for the hidden layer Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

Epoch: 50 Batch Size: 500

In [185]:
batch_size = 500
epochs = 50
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [186]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [187]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.Adam()    # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(128,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu"))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [188]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

In [189]:
model_1.compile(loss=bce_logits_loss, optimizer=optimizer, metrics=["accuracy"])
In [190]:
class_weights = {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0}
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , class_weight=class_weights, batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs)
end=time.time()

0 1 [64] [relu] 30 1000 sgd - - - - 0.210152 0.209642 0.944500 0.9445 6.95
1 1 64 relu 30 1000 sgd 1:5 - - - 0.567296 0.289252 0.944500 0.9445 7.65

4 2 128,64 relu,relu 50 500 adam 0.5:9 - - - 0.184864 0.192104 0.978938 0.9755 20.09

Observations¶

Observations and notes for Model 4:

Model Architecture: This model retains the deeper architecture introduced in Model 3:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). 2 hidden layers: the first with 128 neurons and relu activation, and the second with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True).

Training Configuration:

Epochs: 50 Batch Size: 500 Optimizer: Adam (changed from SGD in Model 3) Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True) Class Weights: {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0} (same as Model 3)

Performance Metrics (after 50 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.185 Validation Loss: ~0.192 Training Accuracy: ~0.979 Validation Accuracy: ~0.9755 Time Taken: ~20.09 seconds

Observations: The switch to the Adam optimizer in Model 4 has significantly improved performance compared to Model 3, and even compared to the earlier models.

Accuracy: Both training and validation accuracies have substantially increased (from 0.91 to 0.97-0.98), indicating that the model is now performing much better overall. Loss: Training and validation losses have drastically decreased, suggesting that the model is learning more effectively and converging towards a better solution. Stability: The loss and accuracy curves (if plotted) would likely show more stable and consistent improvement, contrasting with the more fluctuating or plateaued behavior seen with the SGD optimizer in Model 3. Time: The training time (~20.09 seconds) is comparable to Model 3, but with much better results. This improvement highlights the importance of choosing an appropriate optimizer for neural network training, especially when dealing with deeper architectures and imbalanced datasets where faster and more adaptive optimizers like Adam can be highly beneficial. Model 4 demonstrates a promising balance between higher accuracy and lower loss, making it a stronger candidate for further evaluation.

In [ ]:

Model 5¶

We'll use the same model [ with class weights (1:5)] but with the Adam optimizer and a drop out layer having the following configuration:

1 input, 2 hidden (128,64 nodes), 1 output layers relu, relu activations for the hidden layer Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

Epoch: 50 Batch Size: 500

In [196]:
batch_size = 500
epochs = 50
num_classes=1 #Output is the Target variable alone
In [197]:
# clears the current Keras session, resetting all layers and models previously created, freeing up memory and resources.
tf.keras.backend.clear_session()
In [198]:
#Initializing the neural network

bce_logits_loss = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True)
optimizer = keras.optimizers.Adam()    # defining Adam as the optimizer to be used

model_1 = Sequential()
model_1.add(Dense(128,activation="relu",input_dim = X_train.shape[1]))
model_1.add(Dropout(0.3))
model_1.add(Dense(64,activation="relu"))
model_1.add(Dense(num_classes)) # Removed activation='sigmoid' because from_logits=True is used in the loss function
In [199]:
model_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dropout (Dropout)               │ (None, 128)            │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 64)             │         8,256 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_2 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            65 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Trainable params: 13,569 (53.00 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)
In [200]:
model_1.compile(loss=bce_logits_loss, optimizer=optimizer, metrics=["accuracy"])
In [201]:
class_weights = {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0}
start = time.time()
history = model_1.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val,y_val) , class_weight=class_weights, batch_size=batch_size, epochs=epochs)
end=time.time()

Observations¶

Observations and notes for Model 5:

Model Architecture: This model is based on the deeper architecture from Model 3 and Model 4, but with an added Dropout layer:

1 input layer (matching the 40 features). First hidden layer with 128 neurons and relu activation. Dropout layer with a rate of 0.3 (applied after the first hidden layer). Second hidden layer with 64 neurons and relu activation. 1 output layer with 1 neuron and no activation (logits), suitable for BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True). Training Configuration:

Epochs: 50 Batch Size: 500 Optimizer: Adam Loss Function: BinaryCrossentropy(from_logits=True) Class Weights: {0: 0.5, 1: 9.0} (same as Model 3 and Model 4) Performance Metrics (after 50 epochs):

Training Loss: ~0.203 Validation Loss: ~0.179 Training Accuracy: ~0.975 Validation Accuracy: ~0.970 Time Taken: ~18.28 seconds Observations: The introduction of a Dropout layer in Model 5, while maintaining the same architecture and hyperparameters as Model 4, has resulted in a slight change in performance.

Accuracy: Both training accuracy (0.975) and validation accuracy (0.970) remain very high, comparable to Model 4, but with a minor decrease. This suggests that the model is still performing very well overall. Loss: Training loss (0.203) and validation loss (0.179) are also very low, indicating effective learning. The validation loss is slightly lower than the training loss, which is a good sign, though it fluctuates. Overfitting Reduction: The primary purpose of Dropout is to prevent overfitting. While Model 4 already showed good generalization, the inclusion of Dropout aims to make the model even more robust by randomly deactivating neurons during training. This might explain the slightly lower accuracies compared to Model 4, as the model is encouraged to learn more generalized features. Time: The training time (~18.28 seconds) is slightly faster than Model 4, which could be due to the nature of dropout reducing some computations per epoch. Overall, Model 5 maintains strong performance with high accuracy and low loss, suggesting it's a well-generalized model, potentially more robust to unseen data due to the regularization effect of dropout.

Best Model Selection¶

To choose the best model, we need to consider the specific objective: identifying failures to reduce maintenance costs. Given the high cost of generator replacement (resulting from false negatives), Recall for the 'failure' class (class 1) is the most critical metric, followed by the F1-score and Precision.

The models built so far (Model 0 to Model 5) were primarily evaluated based on overall accuracy and loss. While Model 4 and Model 5 show the highest overall accuracies, these metrics can be misleading in our imbalanced dataset.

Conclusion¶

The Adam optimizer with the Class weights proved effective
